text: The laparoscopic view shows the inflamed appendix with increased vascularity in a ten-year-old male patient. Three ports are used: supra-umbilical, right side, and left side. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to perform the appendectomy, applying traction to the appendix upward and medially while separating the mesoappendix at the base.

The surgeon observes the thickened appendix, approximately 11-12 centimeters in length, in a ten-year-old boy. Using a contralateral port, the surgeon completes the separation at the base of the appendix. A single Western knot is placed at the base using a Y-curl suture technique.

The surgeon pulls the appendix toward the anterior abdominal wall and medially while placing a knot at its base. The visible stump area is below the knot, as the portion above the knot lacks vascularity and will degenerate.

The surgeon uses a 4mm telescope to visualize the removal of the appendix through a 10mm port. The appendix is held longitudinally to prevent slipping, ensuring a secure grip during extraction. The appendix appears thickened, despite its normal-looking consistency, which is typical in smaller children.
The surgeon successfully removes the appendix through the ten mm port, completing the laparoscopic appendectomy procedure.
